coredata.dc:title: A highly selective fluorescent enhancement sensor for Al3+ based nitrogen-doped carbon dots catalyzed by Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Sooksin, Sawarin
Promarak, Vinich
Ittisanronnachai, Somlak
Ngeontae, Wittaya
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorescent material based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was proposed for fabrication of selective turn-on fluorescent sensor for detection of Al3+. The N-CDs were facile synthesized by using sucrose and urea in the presence of Fe3+ as catalyst. The reaction was facile carried out in hot diluted sulfuric acid within only 20 min. The as-prepared N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) exhibited good fluorescent emission, large stroke shift (λex = 311 nm, λem = 428 nm), spherical shape and narrow size distribution with the particle size around 3.9 ± 1.2 nm. The N-CDs exhibited excellence selectivity towards the detection of Al3+. In the presence of Al3+ at trace level, the un-expected fluorescent enhancement was observed while other metal ions did not alter the fluorescent signal. Enhancement of the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs linearly increased with the increasing of the Al3+ concentration in the concentration range of 0.1–5.5 μM with the lowest detection limit of 9.7 nM. The proposed sensor was applied to determine Al3+ in real sample including water samples and bean grain with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical sensors
Fluorescent sensors
Carbon dots
Fluorescent enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CDs catalyzed by Fe3+ (N-CDs)
Application of the sensor in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of the N-CDs
Analytical performance characteristics of the proposed sensor
Application of the proposed sensor in real samples analysis
Comparison of the performance characteristic of the proposed sensor with other related works

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Office of the Higher Education Commission
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Office of the Higher Education Commission

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Thailand Research Fund ( RSA6080006 and RTA6080005 ), and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Office of the Higher Education Commission, Ministry of Education . Appendix A

The following is Supplementary data to this article: Sawarin Sooksin is currently a M.Sc. student in Chemistry under the supervision of associate professor Wittaya Ngeontae, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. She got her Bachelor Degree in Chemistry in 2014 from Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. Her research interests are the optical sensors based nanomaterials. Vinich Promarak is currently professor in Chemistry at School of Molecular Science and Engineering, Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC), Rayong, Thailand. He obtained his D.Phil. degree in Organic Chemistry in 2002 from the Dyson Perrin Laboratory, School of Chemistry, University of Oxford, UK. During his academic research career, he was presented many awards including Thailand’s Young Scientist Award 2007, Outstanding National Researcher Award 2016 (Chemical Sciences and Pharmacy Section), the Thailand Research Fund Senior Research Scholar 2017 and recently CST High Impact Chemist Award 2017 from the Chemical Society of Thailand (CST). His current research interests involve “High-Tech” organic materials that can be used in optoelectronic devices i.e. organic light-emitting diode, perovskite/dye-sensitized solar cells, bulk heterojunction solar cell, sensor, optical switch, organic field-effect transistor. Somlak Ittisanronnachai is currently a research scientist in the Frontier Research Center (FRC), Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC), Rayong, Thailand. She earned her Ph.D. in chemical engineering with Professor Takashi Kyotani at Tohoku University, Sendai, Japan under the Japanese Government (Monbukagakusho) Scholarship. Her research interests include 1) carbon materials and their applications 2) applications of Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). Wittaya Ngeontae is currently an associate professor in Department of Chemistry at Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen, Thailand. He earned his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry in 2008 from Chulalongkorn University under the Development and Promotion of Science and Technology Talent project (DPST). He was the Thailand’s Young Scientist Award in 2013. His current research interests include chemical sensors based optical nanomaterials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) Absorption spectrum (
) and fluorescence spectra (____) of 1.4 mM N-CDs catalyzed by Fe3+ in DI water; the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 311 nm and 428 nm, respectively. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared solutions formed from different reactant compositions including sucrose (CDs), sucrose + Fe3+ (Fe-CDs), sucrose + urea (U-CDs), and sucrose + urea + Fe3+ (N-CDs) in DI water. (c) and (d) TEM images of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+, respectively (prepared in acetic-acetate buffer at a pH of 4.5). (e) and (f) HRTEM images of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+, respectively.
(a) XPS full survey spectrum of N-CDs and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s.

(a) Fluorescence decay profiles of the as-prepared solutions from different reactant compositions including CDs, U-CDs, and N-CDs in DI water. (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, U-CDs and N-CDs synthesized from various concentrations of Fe3+.
Fluorescence spectra of (a) N-CDs and (b) U-CDs in the absence and presence of 3 μM Al3+ in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer at a pH of 4.5.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at various concentrations for the detection of 5 μM Al3+ in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer at a pH of 4.5. (b) The corresponding degree of fluorescence enhancement at various concentrations of N-CDs. (c) Fluorescence spectra of 1.4 mM N-CDs in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer at various solution pH values in the absence and presence of 5 μM Al3+. (d) The corresponding degree of fluorescence enhancement at various solution pH values. (e) Fluorescence spectra of 1.4 mM N-CDs in the absence and presence of 5 μM Al3+ in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer at various equilibration times. (f) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity as a function of the equilibration time.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of 1.4 mM N-CDs in the presence of 5 μM of various cations in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer at a pH of 4.5 and (b) the corresponding degree of fluorescence enhancement (F-F0).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of 1.4 mM N-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Al3+ in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer at a pH of 4.5 and (b) the corresponding calibration curve.
Determination of Al3+ in drinking water samples and tap water samples by using the proposed sensor (n = 3) and the ICP-OES technique (n = 3).
Determination of Al3+ in red beans sample and black beans sample by using the proposed sensor (n = 3) and the ICP-OES technique (n = 3).
Comparison of the merits of Al3+ sensors fabricated from various types of nanomaterials.

row.entry: CDs from ascorbic acid and glycol
CDs from citric acid and methionine
CDs from citric acid and diethylenetriamine
N-CDs from sucrose and urea catalyzed by Fe3+

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *MSA-AgAuNCs = Mercaptosuccinic-Silver Gold alloy nanoclusters, Tyrosine-AuNCs = Tyrosine-stabilized gold nanoclusters, GSH-AuNCs = Glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters, DTT-CuNCs = Dithiothreitol-capped copper nanoclusters, N-GQDs = Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, Salpn-ONPs = N,N’-propylenebis(salicylimine) organic nanoparticles, FONPs = Fluorescent organic nanoparticles, GSH-AgNPs = Silver nanoparticles stabilized by reduced glutathione, Triazole ether-AuNPs = Triazole–ether functionalized gold nanoparticles, AA-AuNPs = Ascorbic acid capped gold nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: A highly selective fluorescent enhancement sensor for Al3+ based nitrogen-doped carbon dots catalyzed by Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Chemistry Research Center, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen, 40002, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Molecular Science and Engineering, Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology, Wang Chan, Rayong, 21210, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Molecular Science and Engineering
Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Frontier Research Center (FRC), Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology, Wang Chan, Rayong, 21210, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescent material based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was proposed for fabrication of selective turn-on fluorescent sensor for detection of Al3+. The N-CDs were facile synthesized by using sucrose and urea in the presence of Fe3+ as catalyst. The reaction was facile carried out in hot diluted sulfuric acid within only 20 min. The as-prepared N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) exhibited good fluorescent emission, large stroke shift (λex = 311 nm, λem = 428 nm), spherical shape and narrow size distribution with the particle size around 3.9 ± 1.2 nm. The N-CDs exhibited excellence selectivity towards the detection of Al3+. In the presence of Al3+ at trace level, the un-expected fluorescent enhancement was observed while other metal ions did not alter the fluorescent signal. Enhancement of the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs linearly increased with the increasing of the Al3+ concentration in the concentration range of 0.1–5.5 μM with the lowest detection limit of 9.7 nM. The proposed sensor was applied to determine Al3+ in real sample including water samples and bean grain with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays fluorescent probes based optical nanomaterials have gained considerable attention in chemical sensor research area due to their special optical properties that can not be observed from its bulk material. A zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) is one of a novel member of optical nanomaterials and the most popular carbon-based nanomaterials. CDs possess good fluorescence emission due to size and quantum confinement effects. Thus, the CDs have been applied for the fabrication of efficient fluorescent sensors. This is due to its unique properties such as easy to prepare, excellent water solubility, robust chemical inertness, high quantum yield, and facile to further modification with various organic, polymeric, inorganic or biological species. In addition, CDs can overcome some problems of the semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) due to their outstanding optical properties especially their cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility, environmental friendliness and tunable emissions, ease of functionalization. Thus, the CDs may be the new candidate to replace semiconductor QDs in the next future.

There have been reported several strategies to improve the fluorescent emission of the CDs. One of the most popular methods is the doping CDs with N, S and N/S by hydrothermal between the carbon source with the compounds containing these atoms. Among these doping atom, the electron-rich N seem to be the most commonly used heteroatom to improve some properties of the CDs including provide more active sites, reduce the surface energy, provide large surface area and enhanced catalytic activity of CDs. Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) have been reported by using different type of carbon source and N-containing compounds. In addition, some intrinsic properties of the CDs such as optical characteristics and surface features can be tuned by doping N nitrogen [1–3]. The N-CDs possesses excellence fluorescent emission which is stem from the size of N and C are not much difference and N contains five valence electrons for bonding with C [4].

Very recently, Jin, J. and co-worker [5] proposed a feasible and effective method by ferric ion as the catalysis for the first time to controlling N speciation which can improve photoluminescence of the N-CDs. In that work, N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal of l-histidine in the presence of Fe3+. They can elucidate true and clear mechanism of fluorescence improvement. According to the catalytic effect of Fe3+, the as-prepared N-CDs possesses more pyrrolic N and less pyridinic N which was the reason why the quantum yield can be improved without any surface passivation. In addition, the graphitic core was more organized and increased a number of N moieties on the N-CDs surface which can also facilitate photoluminescence enhancement. From this synthesis strategy, selective fluorescent sensor based on the catalytic effect of Fe3+ for generation of N-CDs was developed for Fe3+ detection with satisfactory result.

Determination of Al3+ is very important in various aspects such as in the clinical diagnosis for identification of dementia, myopathy, renalosteodystrophy, Parkinson and Alzheimer's diseases [6–10]. In addition, Al3+ can be basically found at trace level in the drinking water due to the Al3+ always use as the flocculating agent in water treatment process [11]. More importantly, the aluminium phosphide is the most commonly used to protect grain held in stores. Al3+ can be released when this compound contact with moisture and contaminate on the grain surface. Generally, aluminum (Al) content in the sample can be simply determined by inductively couple plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) which is the powerful technique for trace metal analysis. However, this technique possesses high operation cost, time-consuming, require highly trained personnel and sophisticated instrumentation, and not suitable to real-time monitoring.

From these drawbacks, fluorescent chemosensor seems to be an alternative approach for the detection of trace Al3+ because of their potential applications in several aspects [12–15]. However, it is not easy to design the fluorescent chemosensor for selective detection of Al3+ due to the lack of spectroscopic characteristics, poor coordination ability of Al3+ and strong hydration ability [16–19]. There have been reported several fluorescent chemosensors based Schiff bases compounds designed for the selective detection of Al3+. Although, there has been reported that the carbon dots synthesized from hydrothermal between ascorbic acid and glycol can be used as the fluorescent chemosensor for detection of Al3+ with the detection limit around 0.39 μM [20]. However, searching for new chemosensors for selective detection of Al3+ at trace concentration level with improved selectivity is still a challenging issue.

Herein, we demonstrate a facile strategy for the synthesis of N-CDs by pyrolyzing sucrose with urea in hot sulfuric acid and using Fe3+ as a catalyst. The as-prepared N-CDs possess spherical shape and narrow size distribution with a strong fluorescence emission when exciting under UV light. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N-CDs can be selectively enhanced by Al3+ while other cations did not significantly alter the fluorescence spectrum. Based on this phenomenon therefore a new fluorescent sensor for detection of Al3+ based on the synthesized N-CDs was fabricated. The possible sensing mechanism was deeply studied by several techniques for explanation the fluorescent enhancement phenomenon. In order to achieve the sensitive method for detection of Al3+, thus the sensor characterization and optimization were studied in details. The application of this sensor in real sample analysis including water and bean grain was also described. This approach will be the simple, fast, and low cost method comparing with atomic spectroscopy instrumentation.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Iron (III) nitrate was obtained from Carlo Erba Reagents. Sucrose and acetic acid were purchased from QReC. Urea and sodium hydroxide were received from Ajax. Sulfuric acid was obtained from Fluka. Aluminium sulfate was purchased from Sigma. Deionized water (DI) with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was obtained from a Millipore water purification system.

All of fluorescence spectra were recorded by using a RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The slit width used for both excitation and emission was 5 nm. Absorption spectrum of the N-CDs solution was recorded on a Agilent HP 8453 spectrophotometer (Agilent, Germany). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the N-CDs were carried out on a Tecnai G2-20 (FEI, Netherlands) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Bright field high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (e.g., observation of lattice spacing and size of nanoparticles) were performed with a JEOL JEM ARM200F transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The pH of N-CDs solutions was measured using a UB-10 Ultra Basic pH meter (Denver Instrument). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed with a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments, FS5). The functional groups in N-CDs were studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) with a Bruker TENSOR27 spectrometer. The percent of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in N-CDs were measured using CHN analyzer (Thermo Fisher 2000, USA). An inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP–OES, Optima 2100 DV, USA) was used to determine total Al content in the real samples. The X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CDs catalyzed by Fe3+ (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Into a 10 mL test tube, 1000 μL of 1.0 M sucrose solution was sequentially mixed with 1000 μL of 2.5 M H2SO4, 1000 μL of 0.7 M urea solution and 500 μL of 0.7 M Fe3+, respectively. Then, the mixture solution was covered with foil and gently homogenized before being left to heat at 100 °C in an oil bath for 20 min. After heating, the brown solution was made up to a final volume of 7.0 mL using DI water.

Into the 10 mL volumetric flask contained appropriate volume of 1 mM standard Al3+ solution and diluted by deionized (DI) water. Then, 100 μL of the as-prepared N-CDs was added into the diluted Al3+ solution. Next, 500 μL of 6 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5 was added to the solution of Al3+. The mixture was made to a final volume of 10.00 mL with deionized water and incubated at room temperature for 5 min before measuring the fluorescence spectrum. The fluorescence spectra were recorded upon the excitation wavelength of 311 nm.

Into the 10 mL volumetric flask contained 50 μL of 1 mM metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ added and diluted with DI water. Then, 100 μL of the synthesized N-CDs solution was added into each metal ions solution. The mixture solutions were buffered by adding 500 μL of 6 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5 and adjusted to the final volume of 10.00 mL with DI water. This solution was then incubated at room temperature for 5 min before recording the individual fluorescence spectrum by using excitation wavelength of 311 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed sensor, determination of Al3+ in real samples by the proposed sensor including drinking water, tap water, red bean grain and black beans grain were evaluated. The drinking water sample was obtained from local supermarket and the tap water was collected using polyethylene bottles. The water samples were used immediately without pretreatment. Into the 10 mL volumetric flask contained each type of water samples, appropriate volumes of 1 mM standard Al3+ solution were spiked. Then, 100 μL of the as-prepared N-CDs solution was added into the sample solution following by the addition of 500 μL of 6 M acetic-acetate buffer solution pH 4.5 and adjusted to the final volume by the water samples. The solution in the volumetric flask was mixed thoroughly and left for 30 min before recording the fluorescence spectra.

To determine the Al3+ in bean grain samples, the following procedure was carried out. Red beans grain and black beans grain were obtained from local supermarket. The bean grains were weighted around 0.5 g and spiked with appropriate volume of 0.5 and 1.5 μM Al3+ standard solution. The spiked bean grains were individually soaked in 25 mL of DI water for 3 min. Into the 10 mL volumetric flask contained the soaked solution, 100 μL of the as-prepared N-CDs was added following by 500 μL of 6 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5 and adjusted to a final volume of 10.00 mL by the soaked solution. The solution was mixed thoroughly and left for 5 min before recording the fluorescence spectra. The accuracy and precision of the proposed sensor were evaluated by the determination of spiked samples at two different concentrations under the optimized condition. In addition, the concentrations of Al3+ in the water samples, bean grain samples and its spiked samples were also determined by ICP-OES for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs were synthesized by using sucrose and urea as a carbon source and nitrogen source, respectively. The reaction was simply carried out in diluted H2SO4 acid in the presence of Fe3+ as the catalyst. The mixture solution was heated at 100 °C for 20 min in normal atmospheric pressure. The optical characteristics in term of absorbance and fluorescent emission were studied and the results are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited broad absorption spectrum with the characteristic absorption peak at 284 nm according to the π–π* transition of the nanocarbon [21] and the onset wavelength at around 350 nm. The synthesized N-CDs showed the highest fluorescent emission peaked center at 428 nm when excitation at 311 nm. The fluorescence spectrum exhibited narrow spectrum shape signified that the obtained particles possesses monodisperse particle.

In order to study the role of each composition in the synthesized of N-CDs, the different synthesis compositions were evaluated under the optimized condition for the synthesis of N-CDs. The absorption and emission spectrum of each synthesized products were measured as the results showed in Fig. 1(b). The results exhibited that very weak fluorescent emission can be observed if the reaction contained only sucrose, sucrose + Fe3+, sucrose + urea (without Fe3+), in the H2SO4 solution. It should be noted that in the absence of Fe3+, the N can be doped on the CDs structure as can be seen from the blue shift spectrum (from 518 to 414 nm) compare to the system without urea. However, the obtained product provided very weak fluorescent intensity. On the other hand, strong fluorescence spectrum can be observed only in the case of the reaction contained sucrose and urea in the presence of Fe3+. This result can be confirmed the important role of the Fe3+ in the synthesis of N-CDs. This is due to the Fe3+ acted as the catalyst in the doping N into the structure of CDs according to the increasing number of pyrrolic N and reducing of pyridinic N moieties as discovered by Jin, J. and co-worker [5]. In addition, the optical characteristic of the CDs and N-CDs were significantly different signified the successfully doped N into the structure of the CDs.

The morphology of the as prepared N-CDs was studied by TEM as the results shown in Fig. 1(c). The obtained N-CDs possessed spherical shape and narrow size distribution with the average particle sized of 3.9 ± 1.2 nm (N = 149). In addition, a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation can be observed. From TEM image exhibited that the synthesized N-CDs were of high quality particle characteristics.

The XPS experiment was carried out in order to confirm the composition and the structure of the synthesized N-CDs as the results exhibited in Fig. 2 . The full scan XPS spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. 2(a)) showed peak at 285, 399 and 530 eV which corresponding to carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), and oxygen (O 1s) elements, respectively. Particularly, the C 1s XPS spectrum as shown in Fig. 2(b) was resolved into four peaks at 283.5, 285.0, 286.4, and 287.9 eV corresponding to four types of carbon bonds: CC or CC, CN, CO and CO or CN respectively [22]. In addition, the N 1s spectrum of N-CDs as shown in Fig. 2(c) composed of three peaks at 398.9, 400.0, and 401.0 eV corresponded to pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic N, respectively [5]. These results demonstrated that nitrogen had reacted with carbon and successfully doped in the particles. Moreover, for O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) possessed three peaks at 530.4, 531.3 and 532.4 eV which were assigned to CO, CO and CO, respectively [23]. From the XPS results, it can be concluded that the synthesized N-CDs composed of C, N and O while no significantly peak according to the Fe can be observed. This result indicated that the Fe3+ was not associated in the N-CDs particle.

In addition, the element composition of the synthesized N-CDs was determined by CHN analysis. The result exhibited that the N-CDs composed of 17.42% C, 3.91% of H and 2.73% N. The result exhibited that there was very small amount of N in the N-CDs, however, it can significant affect to the fluorescent emission efficiency. This result corresponded well with the XPS results.

Furthermore, the EDX spectrum (under TEM experiment) was recorded in order to investigate whether the Fe3+ exist in the N-CDs or not and the result showed in Fig. S1 (ESI). The EDX spectra of the N-CDs exhibited that the as-prepared N-CDs composed of C, N and O while no peak according to the Fe can be observed. This result indicated that the Fe3+ was not associated in the particle. This may be due to the Fe3+ acted as catalyst for doping N to the structure of CDs. It should be noted that the obtained results corresponded well with XPS results.

Moreover, the relaxation behavior of each synthesized product from different reactant compositions were studied by fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy. The fluorescence decay profile and their fitting parameters are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and Table S1 (ESI), respectively. The results exhibited that the relaxation profile of each synthesized products were different. The CDs obtained from un-doped system provided the shortest fluorescence lifetime. However, the longer fluorescence lifetime can be observed when doping with urea. The N-CDs from the system catalyzed by Fe3+ exhibited different lifetime with the un-catalyzed system signified that the CDs were doped with N and the different doped structures can be obtained from two different systems. It should be noted that the entire synthesized product possesses short lifetime signified that the possible mechanism of the radioactive recombination nature of excitations [24].

The functional groups of the as-prepared products (after freeze dried) from different reactant compositions including CDs, U-CDs, N-CDs were studied by using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs catalyzed by Fe3+ showed significant differ pattern form the CDs and U-CDs. This revealed that the different products can be obtained from the different reactant compositions system. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs displayed four prominent peaks. The peak at about 3290 cm−1 corresponded to the OH stretching mode. The peak at about 1641 cm−1 indicated the presence of carbonyl (CO) groups. The peak at about 1361 cm−1 was due to the CC stretch of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, while the peak at about 1043 cm−1 indicated CO bond [25]. These results showed that the CDs have carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface. However, the FT-IR spectrum of U-CDs and N-CDs catalyzed by Fe3+ showed significant differ pattern form the CDs. The synthesized U-CDs and N-CDs showed a main absorption band of OH stretching vibration at 3329 cm−1 and the peaks at 2923 cm−1 are related to the CH bond stretching vibrations. The bending vibration of the CO bond appeared at 1011 cm−1 [26]. Moreover, the mixed band at 1702 and 1624 cm−1 were assigned to CO and CN and the absorption bands at 1339 and 1468 cm−1 were assigned to CN and CNH, respectively [5,26]. This further confirmed the successful doping of N atoms in CDs. In addition, comparison of FT-IR spectrum between U-CDs and N-CDs (which synthesized by using different concentrations of Fe3+), the FT-IR patterns reveal that with more Fe3+ catalysis, the ratio of CN (1339 cm−1) to CNH (1468 cm−1) was significantly lower, suggested that in the Fe3+ promoted the pyrrolic N in the synthesized N-CDs [5].

Basically, the fluorescent emission of the CDs is relatively weak, however doping with N, N/S or S into the structure of the CDs can be improved the fluorescent emission of the CDs. In addition, the doped CDs can provide a new functionality that can be interact with the target specie in the selective direction. Actually, the designation of the chormophore/fluorophore for selective detection of Al3+ by spectrometry is a challenging issue due to the lack of spectroscopic characteristics, poor coordination ability of Al3+ and strong hydration ability [16–19]. Basically, most of the reported chormophore/fluorophore for selective sensing of Al3+ are the Schiff base compounds that contained CN bond. This may be due to the compatibility of hard-soft acid-base between Al3+ and the N of the CN moiety. In order to find the way for detection of Al3+ by using chemosensor based CDs thus it may be mimicked the structure with conventional chemosensors by doping with N into the structure of CDs. In addition, as it has been reported that the using of Fe3+ as catalyst can promote the number of pyrrolic N in the CDs structure. Therefore, it can be hypothesized that the present of pyrrolic N in the CDs structure will play an important role in the interaction with Al3+ in a selective direction and can be affected to the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs via aggregation induce emission strategy.

The fluorescent response of the as-prepared N-CDs and its equivalent product (synthesis from un-catalyzed system; U-CDs) toward the detection of Al3+ were studied in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5. The results are exhibited in Fig. 4 . It should be noted that the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs can be affected by the solution pH. In 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5, large blue shift spectrum with relatively weaker fluorescent intensity and broader spectrum compared with the as-prepared N-CDs (Fig. 1(a)) can be observed. This may be due to the dissociation of the carboxylic moieties on the surface of N-CDs. Beside, the solution pH showed less effect towards the fluorescence spectrum of the U-CDs. However, the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs peaked at 375 nm was drastically enhanced upon the addition of Al3+ with blue shift and different spectral pattern compare to the N-CDs in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer solution pH 4.5. This can be confirmed that the Al3+-induced photoluminescence enhancing of the N-CDs solution. On the other hand, there was no any spectrum change can be observed when adding Al3+ into the solution of U-CDs under the same condition. These results strongly confirmed that different CDs structures were obtained from the different synthesis systems (catalyzed and un-catalyzed by Fe3+). In the catalyzed by Fe3+ system, the obtained CDs possessed the functionality that can interact with Al3+ and resulting in the fluorescent enhancement. The fluorescent enhancement is attributed to the high affinity of electrostatic and coordination interactions [27] between Al3+ and appropriate functional groups on the surface of N-CDs. From this observation, thus the N-CDs can potentially be used as a sensor probe for detection of Al3+ via fluorescent turn-on strategy.

As mentioned in the previous section, the as-prepared N-CDs catalyzed Fe3+ can potentially be used as a fluorescent probe for Al3+ detection. This is due to the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs in acidic buffer solution can be drastically enhanced by the Al3+. According to the report by Jin, J. and co-worker [5], the synthesis system catalyzed by Fe3+ can promote the pyrrolic N specie on the CDs structure. It can be expected that the Al3+ can induce the aggregation between N-CDs particles via the interaction with the pyrrolic N moieties on the surface of N-CDs. This is therefore enhanced the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs via the well known aggregation induce emission mechanism according to the restriction of each particles motion [28,29]. Basically, aggregation of organic small molecules always depends on the exist functional groups in the aggregation moieties [30]. It should be notified that the U-CDs did not exhibit this phenomenon. This observation signified that the obtained functional groups from the catalyzed system (by Fe3+) played an important role in the fluorescent enhancement by Al3+.

The interaction between Al3+ and N-CDs was studied by measuring the absorbance of the N-CDs. The absorbance of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+ were shown in Fig. S2 (ESI). The result showed that the increasing of the absorption spectra of the N-CDs with the same spectrum pattern can be observed upon the addition of Al3+ mV. This indicated that there was no reaction occurred on the N-CDs upon the addition of Al3+. The increasing of the absorbance may be according to the aggregation of the particle that can both absorb and scatter the incidence light source. This results can be supported the aggregation of the N-CDs in the presence of Al3+.

In order to investigate the aggregation of the particle, the TEM images of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of the Al3+ were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 1(d). The results exhibited that the almost spherical N-CDs with the average size of about 4 nm aggregated together with other particles, yielded the bigger particles and non-uniform shape possessed the size around 10 nanometers. This result strongly evidenced that the Al3+ induced the aggregation of the N-CDs particles and promoted the fluorescence enhancement.

The Fig. 1(e) and (f) exhibited HRTEM images of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+, respectively. The as-prepared N-CDs have an obvious spacing of the adjacent lattice planes at 0.20 nm, which corresponds to the (102) facet of graphitic carbon [31,32]. In the presence of Al3+, the N-CDs were aggregated and showed the spacing of the adjacent lattice planes at 0.20 nm as well as the as-prepared N-CDs. The results demonstrated that the aggregation of N-CDs did not affect to the spacing of the adjacent lattice planes of the as-prepared N-CDs.

Furthermore, in order to study the change of the size of the N-CDs, thus the hydrodynamic diameter of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+ was measured as the results showed in Fig. S3 (ESI). The results exhibited that in the presence of Al3+ the average hydrodynamic diameter of N-CDs increased from 2.0 nm to 3.5 nm. In addition the dispersity of the particle increased after the addition of Al3+ indicating that the size and shape of the particles were changed due to the aggregation of N-CDs.

In order to prove that the Al3+ interact with the N-CDs particles thus the EDX spectrum (under TEM experiment) of the N-CDs before and after addition of Al3+ were measured. The EDX spectra of the N-CDs are shown in Fig. S4 (ESI). It can be seen that there was no Al peak can be observed on the N-CDs particles. However, the signal of Al can be observed on the N-CDs particle after the addition of Al3+. The EDX results signified that the Al3+ was incorporated in the N-CDs particles which can be supported the interaction of Al3+ with the N-CDs particles.

In order to confirm electrostatic affinity of Al3+ to N-CDs, zeta potential of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+ were investigated and the results showed in Table S2 (ESI). The as-prepared N-CDs in DI water exhibited average zeta potential value of +23.37 mV. This may be due to the N-CDs was synthesized in H2SO4 solution, thus the protonated product can be obtained. However, when the running solution assay was conducted in the 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5 thus the zeta potential of the N-CDs in buffer solution was evaluated and found to be −17.43 mV. The negative potential may be due to the dissociation of the carboxylic moieties on the N-CDs surface. In addition, the zeta potential inversely back to the positive value of +13.04 mV upon the addition of Al3+. This was due to the Al3+ interact with the surface of N-CDs particles and therefore the positively charge on the surface of N-CDs can be recorded. This result corresponded well with other evidences that the Al3+ was associated on the surface of N-CDs.

The NMR experiments were carried out in order to investigate the interactions between the N-CDs and Al3+ as the results showed in Fig. S5 (ESI). The NMR spectrum of the N-CDs (in D2O) exhibited the group of proton signal at around 4.0–2.5 ppm. After the addition of Al3+ into the same solution, the different pattern of proton signal can be observed. This is the strong evidence that the Al3+ possesses some interactions with the N-CDs.

Fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of the Al3+ was carried out in order to investigate the relaxation profile of both systems. The fluorescence lifetime profiles were shown in Fig. S6 (ESI). The addition of Al3+ ions into the solution of N-CDs causes almost no changes in the lifetime decay of N-CDs suggested that the electron recombination pathway of the N-CDs did not change upon the interaction with Al3+. This result indicated that the fluorescent emission was obtained from the same fluoresce species but with the better emission efficiency. This results supported the aggregation induce emission mechanism which the increasing of the fluorescent emission is according to restriction of intramolecular motion of the same fluoresce species.

From all of the obtained evidences strongly confirmed that the fluorescent enhancement of the N-CDs was due to the aggregation induce emission mechanism cause by Al3+. Thus, this sensor can be potentially applied for the detection of Al3+. The merits of the sensor for the detection of Al3+ were investigated in the next section.
The following results are the parameters that may likely affect to the sensitivity of Al3+ detection by the N-CDs. The parameters were studied and optimized to obtain the best detection sensitivity.

Basically, the concentration of the sensor probe plays an important role in the detection sensitivity and working linear range. In addition, for the nanoparticles based fluorescent sensor, the concentration of nanoparticles can be affect to the optical phenomenon such as the particle aggregation and self absorption at the higher probe concentration. Thus the concentration of the N-CDs has to be studied and optimized. The experiment was carried out by varying the concentration of N-CDs ranging from 0.7 to 2.8 mM while fixing other components and measuring the fluorescence spectrum before and after adding 5 μM Al3+ in 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5. The fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+ and their corresponded degree of fluorescent change were exhibited in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), respectively. The result showed that a little bit change in the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs itself can be observed whole the studied concentration range. However, the dramatically increment of the fluorescent intensity can be observed when adding Al3+ for all studied concentration (of N-CDs) levels. The maximum degree of fluorescent change can be obtained when using 1.4 mM N-CDs. The increasing of the N-CDs concentrations beyond this level did not provide better enhancement response. This result signified that the excess number of N-CDs was not necessary, on the other hand, decreased the degree of fluorescent enhancement. Thus, the concentration of N-CDs at 1.4 mM was chosen for the further studied.

The pH of the detection medium is basically investigated when fabrication of chemical sensor in aqueous solution. This is due to the sensor probe basically composed of acid-base sensitive moieties which can affect to the fluorescent intensity of the sensor probe. In addition, the solution pH can affect to the interaction between target analyte and the sensor probe which directly resulted to the detection sensitivity. Thus, the effect of the solution pH was therefore studied and optimized. The experiment was carried out by varying the pH of the 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer from 3.5 to 5.5 while other parameters were fixed. The fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs at various solution pHs in the absence and presence of 5 μM Al3+ and their corresponded degrees of fluorescent enhancement were exhibited in Fig. 5(c) and (d), respectively. The results showed that the solution pH can be directly affected to the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs. When the solution pH increased (to be more basic) the fluorescence spectra were blue-shifted from 428 nm to 375. This may be due to the dissociation of the carboxylic moieties of the N-CDs. Furthermore, the fluorescent intensity at 375 nm dramatically increased in the presence of only 5 μM of Al3+ for all studied pHs. The degree of the fluorescent enhancement (F-F0) measured at 375 nm were increased while increasing the solution pHs and reached the maximum value at the pH 4.5. This result indicated that the solution pH can affect to the interaction between N-CDs and Al3+. The lower degree of fluorescent enhancement at low solution pH may be due to the protonation of the donor side. Thus, lower degrees of fluorescent enhancement were obtained. In order to obtain the best detection sensitivity, the 0.3 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.5 was chosen for the further studied.

Basically, immediately measurement upon the addition sensor probe to the sample solution is ideally needed for the fabrication of new chemical sensor. However, in fact there needed some equilibration period to obtain the stable signal. Thus, the equilibration time for detection of Al3+ by N-CDs was studied in order to find the shortest analysis time with the highest detection sensitivity. The experiment was carried out by varying the time after mixing between Al3+ and the N-CDs under the optimized condition. The fluorescent intensities of the N-CDs at 375 nm in the absence and presence of 5 μM Al3+ were recorded as a function of equilibration time in the period of 0–60 min and the results are shown in Fig. 5(e) and (f). The results exhibited that the N-CDs possessed very stable fluorescent intensity whole the studied range. Moreover, upon the addition of Al3+, the fluorescent intensity immediately increased. However, the fluorescent intensity can reach steady state equilibrium after adding Al3+ into the solution of N-CDs for 5 min. This result revealed that fast response time can be obtained and very short equilibrium time was required for the analysis. Therefore, in order to obtain the best sensor sensitivity with the shortest analysis time, the equilibrium time of 5 min was used for further studied.

Selectivity of the proposed sensor is a crucial issue in the fabrication of new chemical sensors. It is the most important characteristic of the sensor. Ideally, the changing of the sensor signal must be resulted from only target species which is directly affected to the accuracy of the sensor. For detection of Al3+, the response toward other cations especially cation possesses charge 3+ is the challenging issue. The proposed sensor must be identified the different between other cations and between the cation possesses the same charge. As mentioned in the previous section, it is very difficult to design the chemosensors for selective with Al3+. Thus, the discovery of the new chemosensors for selective detection of Al3+ is still researching.

In order to demonstrate the selectivity of the proposed sensor, response of the proposed sensor towards the possible interfering compounds were investigated. The response from other metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and various amino acids towards the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs were studied under the optimized condition for the detection of Al3+. The fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs in the presence of various cations and their degree of fluorescent enhancement are shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b), respectively. It was clearly seen that the fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs was enhanced only in the presence of Al3+. On the other hand, the fluorescence spectra did not show any significant change from the original spectrum when adding other cations and various amino acids (Fig. S9; ESI). The selectivity of the sensor may be resulted from the pyrrolic N donor atom from the using of Fe3+ as a catalyst for doping N atom to the CDs structure as described in the previous section. The pyrrolic N moieties are responsible for interaction with Al3+ and resulting in the particle aggregation which promoted the fluorescent enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance characteristics of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the analytical performance characteristics of the N-CDs to be used as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Al3+, the relationship between the concentration of Al3+ and the degree of fluorescent enhancement (F-F0) was studied. The experiment was carried out by varying the concentration of Al3+ in the concentration range of 0–8 μM under the optimized condition which described in the previous section. The fluorescent intensity at 375 nm was chosen for quantitative analysis. The fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs in the present of various concentration of Al3+ were recorded and its calibration curve was constructed as the results shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b), respectively. The results showed that the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs linearly increased when increasing the concentration of Al3+ without any spectral change. This indicated that the increasing of the number of the same fluoresce specie at all studied concentration levels. The linear relationship between the concentration of Al3+ and the degree of fluorescent enhancement (F-F0) can be obtained in the range of 0.1–5.5 μM. The linear regression equation was found to be [F-F0] = 3422.89 × [Al3+, μM] + 209.63 with the excellence correlation coefficient (r2) of 0.9969. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were determined according to the concentration of Al3+ giving the fluorescent intensity F0 + (3 × standard deviation of F0) and F0 + (10 × standard deviation of F0), respectively where F0 is the fluorescent intensity of the N-CDs without Al3+. The LOD and LOQ of the proposed sensor were calculated to be 9.7 nM and 172.7 nM, respectively. This result suggested that the proposed sensor exhibited highly sensitivity for the detection of trace Al3+. Hence, this sensor can possibly be applied to the detection of trace Al3+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed sensor in real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
From the results exhibit in the previous section, the proposed sensor has a highly potential to be applied for detection of Al3+ in real samples. Thus in this work, two types of real samples including water samples and natural grain bean samples were chosen to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed sensor. The analysis results obtained by the proposed sensor were summarized in Tables 1 and 2
for water samples and grain bean samples, respectively. The results exhibited that Al3+ in all samples were not detectable by the proposed sensor. This may be due to the contamination level lower than the LOD of the proposed sensor. However, in order to investigate the accuracy of the proposed sensor, the Al3+ standard solution was spiked at two different concentration levels into the sample solutions and analyzed by the proposed sensor. The % recovery values of 98–107% can be obtained, confirming the accuracy of the sensor. Regarding the precision aspect, % R.S.D. values of three measurements were less than 4% signified that this sensor can provide good precisions. Moreover, in order to confirm the accuracy of the analysis results from the proposed sensor, the same samples solutions were analyzed by the standard inductively couple plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES). The analysis results listed in Tables 1 and 2 for water samples and grain bean samples, respectively. The results exhibited that the Al3+ concentration obtained by the proposed sensor agreed very well with the results from the ICP-OES method. The results from this experiment can be confirmed the reliability of the proposed sensor in real application as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance characteristic of the proposed sensor with other related works
ce:section.ce:para: Several types of optical chemical sensor have been proposed for the detection of Al3+ by utilizing the special optical properties of the nanomaterials which are summarized in Table 3 . From Table 3, it can be seen that there are various types of nanomaterials including carbon dots, metal nanoclusters, and organic nanoparticles have been proposed as the fluorescent chemosensors for detection of Al3+. For the CDs based sensors, there are many types of carbon sources and doping agents have been used for synthesis of CDs. Most of the fluorescent chemosensors for the detection of Al3+ are carried out in the fluorescent enhancement mode. Beside the fluorescent sensor, the Al3+ sensor based on the detection of surface plasmon resonance of the AuNPs have also been proposed. However, most of the sensors showed some interference that basically from the other cations. Most of the reports needed some time for equilibration around 5 min that comparable with the proposed sensor. In addition, most of the reported methods provided a narrow working concentration range with relatively low detection limits at a sub micro-molar level. However, the proposed sensor based N-CDs exhibited superior than most of the reports in term of detection limit at nanomolar level. Especially, the excellence selectivity over other cation can be achieved.

This work has successfully demonstrated a new strategy for a highly selective and sensitive detection of Al3+. A new fluorescent probe based N-doped CDs which was synthesized by carbonization natural carbon (sucrose) and nitrogen (urea) source in the presence of Fe3+ as the catalyst. The N-doped CDs that synthesized from the system catalyzed and non-catalyzed by Fe3+ exhibited different response towards the detection of Al3+. Only the N-CDs catalyzed by Fe3+ can provide fluorescent enhancement in the presence of Al3+, on the other hand, the CDs from un-catalyzed system did not exhibit this behavior. The fluorescent enhancement was according to the aggregation of N-CDs particles induced by Al3+. In addition, excellence selectivity towards Al3+ can be achieved and no interference from other cations can be observed. For quantitative analysis approach, excellence figure of merits can be obtained. The degree of fluorescent enhancement was linearly increased as a function of the Al3+ concentration in sub-micromolar range with the detection limit at nanomolar level. Finally, the proposed Al3+ sensor can be applied to determine Al3+ in water samples and bean grain samples with satisfactory results. This sensor may be the possible elegant tool for detection of Al3+ in other application as well.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Thailand Research Fund (RSA6080006 and RTA6080005), and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Office of the Higher Education Commission, Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.239.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sawarin Sooksin is currently a M.Sc. student in Chemistry under the supervision of associate professor Wittaya Ngeontae, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. She got her Bachelor Degree in Chemistry in 2014 from Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. Her research interests are the optical sensors based nanomaterials.

Vinich Promarak is currently professor in Chemistry at School of Molecular Science and Engineering, Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC), Rayong, Thailand. He obtained his D.Phil. degree in Organic Chemistry in 2002 from the Dyson Perrin Laboratory, School of Chemistry, University of Oxford, UK. During his academic research career, he was presented many awards including Thailand’s Young Scientist Award 2007, Outstanding National Researcher Award 2016 (Chemical Sciences and Pharmacy Section), the Thailand Research Fund Senior Research Scholar 2017 and recently CST High Impact Chemist Award 2017 from the Chemical Society of Thailand (CST). His current research interests involve “High-Tech” organic materials that can be used in optoelectronic devices i.e. organic light-emitting diode, perovskite/dye-sensitized solar cells, bulk heterojunction solar cell, sensor, optical switch, organic field-effect transistor.

Somlak Ittisanronnachai is currently a research scientist in the Frontier Research Center (FRC), Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC), Rayong, Thailand. She earned her Ph.D. in chemical engineering with Professor Takashi Kyotani at Tohoku University, Sendai, Japan under the Japanese Government (Monbukagakusho) Scholarship. Her research interests include 1) carbon materials and their applications 2) applications of Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM).

Wittaya Ngeontae is currently an associate professor in Department of Chemistry at Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen, Thailand. He earned his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry in 2008 from Chulalongkorn University under the Development and Promotion of Science and Technology Talent project (DPST). He was the Thailand’s Young Scientist Award in 2013. His current research interests include chemical sensors based optical nanomaterials.
